quintus_catulus_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: The senate meets in three hours to discuss the Italian question — which is not a question at all, but a demand dressed in the language of appeal. I have thirty minutes. If you've come to ask me to support Drusus's bill, save your breath. If you've come for something else, speak quickly.

options.playerDialogue: I've come to listen. The optimates' position deserves to be heard before I commit to anyone.
I want to understand why you'd risk a war over citizenship. The Italians are going to fight.
I've come because Drusus asked me to open a back-channel to you. He believes compromise is possible.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: A man who listens. Rome could use more of those and fewer tribunes with ambitious legislation. Here is what the populares won't tell you: citizenship for the Italians doesn't mean justice. It means flooding the tribal assemblies with men who owe loyalty to Marius and his veterans' network. It's a power grab wearing a toga of virtue.

options.playerDialogue: I hadn't considered that angle. Go on.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: Let them fight. Rome has defeated every Italian confederation for three centuries. The Samnites, the Marsi — they know Roman steel. What they don't know is Roman law, and giving them citizenship won't teach them. It will dilute the franchise until it means nothing. A Republic where everyone votes is a Republic where no vote matters.

options.playerDialogue: A strong position. What would you do instead?

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: Drusus. The man murders Roman constitutional precedent with one hand and offers an olive branch with the other. What kind of compromise is he proposing? And why should I believe you're not simply gathering intelligence for his faction?

options.playerDialogue: He's willing to limit the tribal distribution. New citizens in eight tribes instead of thirty-five.
Because I'm risking my standing with Drusus by coming here. That should tell you something.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: The question that matters: when Sulla and Marius finally tear this city apart — and they will — which side of the line will you be standing on? I'm not asking for your allegiance. I'm asking for your honesty.

responses.playerDialogue: On the side of constitutional order. Whatever that costs. The laws are either sacred or they're nothing.
On the side that avoids bloodshed. I've seen what civil wars do to cities.
On the winning side. I'm honest about that, at least.
I'll be standing wherever I can do the most good for the most people. Including myself.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I don't know yet. And I think that's the only honest answer right now.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: Good. Principle. That's rarer than you'd think. The optimates need men who believe in the institutions, not just men who benefit from them. I'm building a coalition to block the command transfer to Marius — legally, through the senate. I need voices that the equestrians will trust. Are you one of those voices?

options.playerDialogue: I am. Tell me what arguments to make and to whom.
I can be, but I won't lie to the equestrians. If your position has weaknesses, I need to know them.
I'll support you in the senate. But not against the citizenship bill — only against the command transfer.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: Pragmatism. I won't pretend to admire it, but I recognize its uses.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: One more thing. The Lex Varia — the treason law targeting anyone who supported Italian rights. Some of my colleagues want to use it as a weapon. I think it goes too far. Prosecuting men for political speech makes us no better than the tyrants we claim to oppose. Do you agree?

options.playerDialogue: The Lex Varia is an abomination. Kill it before it kills the Republic's remaining credibility.
Use it surgically. Against Marius's agents, not against every man who spoke for the Italians.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: Half-measures. I understand — you're protecting your position. But understand this: when the crisis comes, half-measures protect no one. I'll remember your face. Whether that helps you or not depends on what you do next.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: A man of principle. Rome's oldest virtue and its rarest commodity. You have my respect, which is worth more than my friendship. Come to the senate meeting tonight — I'll introduce you to the men who matter.

quintus_catulus_convo_0_n11.npcDialogue: Surgical. Cold, but effective. I can work with that. The optimates need both swords and scalpels. You'll be the scalpel. I'll send word when the senate committee convenes.

deltas.reason: Catulus champions you as a man of traditional virtue
Your alignment with the optimates bloc is now public
Stood on principle when expedience was easier
Accepted as a useful instrument of the conservative bloc
Rumors of your optimate connections spread
Offered to wield the Lex Varia with surgical precision
Catulus records you as a possible but uncommitted ally
